court: United States District Court for the District of California
document_citation: JOHNSON v. THE CYANE 1 Alaska Fed. 1, 1 (1870)
summary:
Issue: It is for the latter to determine what work is necessary, and when the labor of the crew or of any member of it is required
Holding: Unknown
Key Facts: It is for the latter to determine what work is necessary, and when the labor of the crew or of any member of it is required. In cases of emergency, growing out of disaster or danger to the ship, the necessity for the labor of the crew may be apparent to all. But there are many occasions when the necessity or expediency of requiring their services may depend on circumstances known only to the master, and as to the force of which he alone can judge.

court: United States Circuit Court for the District of Oregon
summary:
Issue: That the objection may be taken in this court for the first time is clear, from the same authorities
Holding: Unknown
Key Facts: That the objection may be taken in this court for the first time is clear, from the same authorities. In the language of Sprague, J., in The Silver Spring [supra] : “This is a question of the existence of those facts, which will warrant the court in proceeding to decree a forfeiture. In requiring a seizure by the collector, prior to the filing of the libel on the part of the government, the legislature has made that fact a prerequisite to a condemnation, and the plea in this case is like the plea of not guilty to an indictment, and puts in issue all material allegations of the information, and if upon the trial, it does not appear that there was a seizure previously to the filing of the libel, the information is not sustained, and a forfeiture will not be decreed.” Upon a suggestion that the allegation of seizure is immaterial and might be omitted, the learned judge said: “But the information would be defective if this allegation were omitted.” And this is manifestly so under the decision in The Ann, 9 Cranch, 289.

court: United States District Court for the District of Oregon
summary:
Issue: The first mentioned of these sections prescribes the oath or affirmation “to be taken and subscribed by the owner or one of the owners” of a vessel, in order to obtain the registry thereof, in which such owner is required to declare “his or her name and place of abode, and if he or she be the sole owner of such ship or vessel, that such is the case; or if there be another owner or other owners, that there is or are such other owner or owners, specifying his, her or their name or names and place or places of abode, and that he, she or they, as the case may be, so swearing or affirming, is or are citizens of the United States
Holding: Unknown
Key Facts: The first mentioned of these sections prescribes the oath or affirmation “to be taken and subscribed by the owner or one of the owners” of a vessel, in order to obtain the registry thereof, in which such owner is required to declare “his or her name and place of abode, and if he or she be the sole owner of such ship or vessel, that such is the case; or if there be another owner or other owners, that there is or are such other owner or owners, specifying his, her or their name or names and place or places of abode, and that he, she or they, as the case may be, so swearing or affirming, is or are citizens of the United States.” This section also provides: “And in case any of the matters of fact in the said oath or affirmation alleged, which shall be within the knowledge of the party, so swearing or affirming, shall not be true, there shall be a forfeiture of the ship or vessel, together with her tackle, furniture and apparel, in respect to which the same shall have been made.” Section 24 of the act of 1866, above mentioned, provides: “That if any certificate of registry, enrollment or license, ór other record or document granted in lieu thereof, to any vessel, shall be knowingly and fraudulently obtained or used for any vessel not entitled to the benefit thereof, such vessel, with her tackle, apparel and furniture, shall be liable to forfeiture.” Having thus fully stated the pleadings and evidence in the case, I do not deem it necessary to make an extended argument in support of the conclusions of facts already deduced therefrom.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: The applicability and enforceability of British statutes in Pennsylvania before and after its settlement.
Holding: Statutes made in Great Britain before the settlement of Pennsylvania have no force unless they are convenient and adapted to the circumstances of the country. Statutes made since the settlement do not apply unless the colonies are specifically named, or recognized by the legislature or courts through long-standing practice.
Key Facts: The case discusses the legal status of British statutes in colonial Pennsylvania, focusing on the statute of frauds and its partial adoption into Pennsylvania law via an act of assembly. It highlights that certain post-settlement British statutes have been incorporated into Pennsylvania law by legislative recognition or judicial precedent.
Disposition: Unknown

case_name: The Lessee of Hyam and others v. Edwards
court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: The Lessee of Hyam and others v. Edwards 1 Dall. 1, 2 (1759)
summary:
Issue: Admissibility of foreign legal documents (specifically copies of deeds and Quaker birth/death records) as evidence in Pennsylvania courts.
Holding: Foreign legal documents, when properly authenticated through proof before a competent authority (e.g., the Lord Mayor of London), may be admitted as evidence to support property titles or prove personal facts such as death.
Key Facts: The case involved the admissibility of a deed copy and Quaker records' copy, both authenticated in England, used to establish legal claims in Pennsylvania.
Disposition: Allowed foreign documents with proper authentication to be admitted as evidence.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: Bethel v. Lloyd and others 1 Dall. 2, 3 (1759)
summary:
Issue: Whether defendants who are tenants at will can be permitted to give evidence in a partition case when they are not tenants of the freehold.
Holding: The court allowed defendants who were tenants at will and not tenants of the freehold to give evidence in the partition case.
Key Facts: Partition action where some defendants were tenants at will rather than tenants of the freehold. Defendants sought to introduce evidence despite their status as tenants at will.
Disposition: Permitted

case_name: The Lessee of Lewis and Mart Weston v. Thomas Stammers
court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: The Lessee of Lewis and Mart Weston v. Thomas Stammers 1 Dall. 2, 3 (1759)
summary:
Issue: Admissibility of depositions and exemplifications of foreign wills as evidence.
Holding: Depositions taken in a former case with party consent are not admissible in the current cause unless read into evidence previously; an exemplification of a will from England, certified by the prerogative court of Canterbury, is allowed to be admitted as evidence.
Key Facts: Depositions were taken in a previous case but not used in that trial. An English will’s exemplification was accepted for evidentiary purposes.
Disposition: Allow exemplification; disallow depositions.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: Whether goods sent to satisfy a debt can be treated as payment equivalent to money when the monetary value of the goods must first be determined.
Holding: The defendant retained possession and control over the rum, which was sent to satisfy a debt. The court held that until the goods are converted into money, the debtor's obligation remains intact, and the creditor retains an interest in the goods equivalent to the debt owed.
Key Facts: Rum was sent as payment for a debt; monetary value of the rum must be determined before it can settle the debt.
Disposition: Judgment for the defendant.

case_name: The Lessee of Asheton v. Asheton
court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: The Lessee of Asheton v. Asheton 1 Dall. 4, 5 (1760)
summary:
Issue: Interpretation of a testator's intent regarding the inheritance of property by the first son.
Holding: The court upheld the testator’s clear intention that the first son should inherit as specified in the will.
Key Facts: Testator left instructions for his property to be inherited by the first son. Legal challenge questioned this arrangement.
Disposition: Judgment rendered in favor of the interpretation supporting the first son's inheritance.

case_name: The Lessee of Joseph and James Hewes v. Andrew McDowell
court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: The Lessee of Joseph and James Hewes v. Andrew McDowell 1 Dall. 5, 6 (1762)
summary:
Issue: Admissibility of a copy of a warrant of survey under the surveyor-general’s hand in legal proceedings.
Holding: The court ruled that a copy of a warrant of survey, signed by the surveyor-general and directing the deputy-surveyor to conduct the survey, is admissible as evidence.
Key Facts: Unknown
Disposition: The copy of the warrant was admitted into evidence without formal determination or judgment.

case_name: Nixon and Harper v. Long and Plumstead
court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: Nixon and Harper v. Long and Plumstead 1 Dall. 6, 7 (1762)
summary:
Issue: Admissibility of a ship master’s protest as evidence in legal proceedings.
Holding: The court allowed the master's protest to be admitted as evidence, establishing a precedent that was later criticized for being erroneous but remained influential due to judicial practice.
Key Facts: Action by vessel owners against charterers; plea that vessel returned empty and no freight was due; replication that it was due to defendants' default; master’s lone protest admitted in evidence.
Disposition: Unknown

case_name: The Lessee of Fothergill and others v. Christian Stover
court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: The Lessee of Fothergill and others v. Christian Stover 1 Dall. 6, 8 (1763)
summary:
Issue: Admissibility of extraneous documents and surveys in land disputes when the official land office was closed.
Holding: Letters from the proprietor's officers and unofficial surveys were admitted as evidence due to historical precedent and necessity, despite not being officially recorded during periods when the land office was shut.
Key Facts: A plot of survey made by Isaac Taylor in 1720 was allowed as evidence against a later official survey. The earlier survey was deemed valid because it predated a period when the land office was closed and there was settlement and possession established.
Disposition: Affirmed; the court upheld the admission of unofficial documents and surveys.

case_name: Thomas Wallace v. Child and Styles
court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: Thomas Wallace v. Child and Styles 1 Dall. 7, 9 (1763)
summary:
Issue: Admissibility of a witness based on their declaration of being disinterested
Holding: A witness may be sworn and admitted if they declare themselves to be disinterested upon voir dire examination.
Key Facts: Unknown
Disposition: The court ordered the witness to undergo a voir dire examination; upon declaring disinterest, the witness was sworn and admitted.

case_name: The Lessee of Albertson v. Robeson
court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: The Lessee of Albertson v. Robeson 1 Dall. 9, 10 (1764)
summary:
Issue: Whether a court decree based on repealed legislation remains valid if the repeal is unknown to the court at the time of the decree.
Holding: The court held that an unknown legislative repeal did not affect the validity of a prior court decree made under the authority of the repealed statute.
Key Facts: Benjamin Albertson sued Septimus Robeson for ejectment over lands originally owned by Albertson's ancestor. The court had previously decreed the land to Robeson based on an act that was later repealed without notification to Pennsylvania, rendering the repeal unknown at the time of the decree.
Disposition: Judgment in favor of defendant (Robeson).

case_name: The King v. Phillip Henry Rapp
court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: The King v. Phillip Henry Rapp 1 Dall. 9, 11 (1764)
summary:
Issue: Whether a motion to continue a criminal trial due to missing material witnesses should be granted when the defendant's livelihood depends on the outcome.
Holding: The court granted the continuance but emphasized it was not setting a precedent and that trials should not be hurried in any case to avoid injustice.
Key Facts: Defendant, a clergyman, faced an indictment for performing a marriage ceremony when the woman had another husband living. He moved to continue the trial due to missing witnesses crucial to his defense.
Disposition: Continuance granted.

case_name: The King v. Haas and others
court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: The King v. Haas and others 1 Dall. 9, 11 (1764)
summary:
Issue: Whether the court can force the Crown to bring a trial or discharge a defendant from bail without evidence of oppression.
Holding: The court will not compel the Crown to proceed with a trial nor release a defendant on bail unless there is an appearance of oppression.
Key Facts: Defendants moved to either hasten the trial or be released from bail. The court referenced the Habeas Corpus Act of 1785 and another case (Commonwealth v. Prophet) in its ruling.
Disposition: Unknown

case_name: The Lessee of Richardson v. Campbell
court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: The Lessee of Richardson v. Campbell 1 Dall. 10, 12 (1764)
summary:
Issue: Whether the plaintiff can prove imposition on an officer when no such proof is available.
Holding: The court ruled in favor of the defendant as the plaintiff could not prove any imposition on the officer.
Key Facts: Plaintiff failed to provide evidence of imposition on an officer and declined to take a verdict unfavorable, leading to a nonsuit.
Disposition: Nonsuit in favor of the defendant.

case_name: Story and Wharton v. Amos Strettel
court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: Story and Wharton v. Amos Strettel 1 Dall. 10, 12 (1764)
summary:
Issue: Compensation for salvage and damages due to capture
Holding: The court instructed the jury to award compensation only for salvage, charges, and loss of time resulting from the capture.
Key Facts: Plaintiffs sought full recovery; defendant argued partial recovery. Court's instruction favored defendant’s position.
Disposition: Partial victory for plaintiffs; verdict limited to specific compensatory damages.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: Whether a review of a road confirmation decision by justices was a matter of right when requested by an aggrieved party.
Holding: The court held that a review is a matter of right for parties interested, even though it was not explicitly mentioned in the act of assembly at the time.
Key Facts: A person petitioned for a review after a road confirmation decision affected his improved ground. The justices refused to grant the review. The Supreme Court reversed the original judgment due to the refusal of the review.
Disposition: Reversed

case_name: Hugh Davey et ux. v. Peter Turner
court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: Hugh Davey et ux. v. Peter Turner 1 Dall. 11, 14 (1764)
summary:
Issue: Whether a deed executed by a married woman without separate examination is valid.
Holding: The court upheld the validity of the deed based on the principle of 'communis error facit jus' (common error creates law), given that such practices had been longstanding and generally accepted in the province.
Key Facts: A married woman executed a deed without undergoing separate examination, which was customary but not legally required at the time. The court recognized this practice as having historical precedent and judicial acceptance.
Disposition: Judgment for the defendant.

case_name: The Lessee of Thomas v. Horlocker
court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: The Lessee of Thomas v. Horlocker 1 Dall. 14, 16 (1766)
summary:
Issue: Whether a deed is admissible as evidence when one witness is dead, another unknown, and there is no direct testimony regarding possession or other corroborating evidence?
Holding: The court held that the indirect evidence provided by a person familiar with the handwriting of one of the witnesses was sufficient to prove the authenticity of the deed, given its antiquity.
Key Facts: A sixty-three-year-old deed was presented in court. One witness was deceased and another unknown. The plaintiff relied on circumstantial testimony from someone who recognized the handwriting but had no direct knowledge of the deed's execution or possession.
Disposition: Affirmed; the deed was admitted as evidence.

case_name: Boehm and Shitz v. Andrew Engle
court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: Boehm and Shitz v. Andrew Engle 1 Dall. 15, 17 (1767)
summary:
Issue: Whether the Act of 82 Hen. VIII. applies to Pennsylvania and governs property rights involving lives of women.
Holding: The court held that the Act of 82 Hen. VIII. does extend to Pennsylvania and is applicable in determining property rights involving lives of women.
Key Facts: Plaintiffs sued defendant; jury found for plaintiffs after making an allowance for the lives of two women involved in the property dispute.
Disposition: Judgment for plaintiffs.

case_name: John Swift v. Hawkins and others
court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: John Swift v. Hawkins and others 1 Dall. 17, 19 (1768)
summary:
Issue: Whether a defendant can introduce evidence of mistake or lack of consideration to invalidate a bond when there is no court of equity.
Holding: The court allowed defendants to prove the absence of consideration under the plea of payment, due to the lack of an equitable remedy in the province.
Key Facts: Plaintiff sued for debt on a bond. Defendants claimed payment and sought to introduce evidence that there was no valid consideration for the bond. The court permitted this evidence, establishing precedent.
Disposition: Verdict passed for defendants.

case_name: The Lessee of Biddle v. Shippen
court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: The Lessee of Biddle v. Shippen 1 Dall. 19, 21 (1773)
summary:
Issue: Whether the Statute of 21 Jac. I., c. 16, which allows for twenty years’ possession to establish title by prescription, applies in Pennsylvania.
Holding: The Court held that the statute does apply in Pennsylvania.
Key Facts: Unknown
Disposition: Affirmed the applicability of the Statute of 21 Jac. I., c. 16, in Pennsylvania.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: Division of estate
Holding: The estate should be divided.
Key Facts: Mr. Justice Willing and Justice Lawrence ruled that the estate in question should be divided; the plaintiff suffered a nonsuit.
Disposition: Nonsuit

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: Whether a list of purchasers can sufficiently describe the nature of the estate conveyed in a deed when attempting to prove ownership through parol evidence.
Holding: The court held that while a deed’s existence is clear, the nature and extent of the estate it conveys must be proven by admissible evidence. The custom and circumstances of the province allow courts to take judicial notice of certain transactions, but specific facts about the estate must still be supported by evidence.
Key Facts: A list of first purchasers was admitted as evidence; the nature of the estate conveyed in a deed from William Penn was disputed; the court considered provincial custom and historical context.
Disposition: The case went to the jury for determination of the specific facts regarding the nature of the estate, given the insufficiency of the list alone to prove the type of estate.

case_name: Wheeler, Assignee of Baynton, v. Hughes, Executor
court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: Wheeler, Assignee of Baynton, v. Hughes, Executor 1 Dall. 23, 25 (1776)
summary:
Issue: Whether an assignee can be subject to the same defenses that the original creditor could assert against the debtor, particularly regarding defalcations.
Holding: The court held that an assignee takes the bond at their own peril and stands in the same position as the obligee with respect to any defalcation the obligor had against the obligee at the time of assignment or notice thereof.
Key Facts: Hughes seeks a defalcation against Wheeler, who is Baynton's assignee. The court considers whether the Pennsylvania statute governing negotiable instruments extends the same protections as English law for assignees of bonds.
Disposition: Judgment for the defendant (Hughes).

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: Whether it is contempt to pocket the venire and procedural motions regarding continuance and proviso rule.
Holding: Unknown
Key Facts: Plaintiff moved to continue the cause; defendant requested a proviso rule but dropped motion after plaintiff's affidavit of witness absence and service of subpoena. Chief Justice cited legal authorities on contempt for pocketing venire.
Disposition: Unknown

court: Philadelphia Court of Oyer and Terminer
document_citation: Respublica v. Molder et al. 1 Dall. 33, 33 (1778)
summary:
Issue: The right of an accused person charged with high treason to receive a copy of the indictment and the panel of jurors before trial.
Holding: An accused must be provided with a copy of the indictment (excluding witness names) and the jury panel at least one day before the trial upon request by their attorney or agent and payment of reasonable fees.
Key Facts: Unknown
Disposition: The court ruled in favor of the procedural rights for the accused.

court: Philadelphia Court of Oyer and Terminer
summary:
Issue: Whether evidence of a defendant's actions with enemy forces can be admitted in a treason trial when those actions occurred outside the jurisdiction where the trial is held.
Holding: The court allowed evidence of overt acts committed outside the trial county if an overt act is proven within the trial county, as corroborative evidence may be given for acts committed elsewhere.
Key Facts: Defendant was seen parading with enemy forces in Philadelphia. Defense argued that actions alone without a traitorous intent did not constitute treason and must be tried where they occurred.
Disposition: Unknown

court: Philadelphia Court of Oyer and Terminer
summary:
Issue: Whether the indictment sufficiently alleges an overt act of treason when it charges the defendant with taking a commission from the British king and granting passes in enemy-occupied territory without specifying the contents or details of these acts.
Holding: The court held that the indictment was sufficient, as it alleged the defendant took a commission under the British king and had power to grant passes into and out of Philadelphia when it was occupied by the enemy, which are overt acts of treason.
Key Facts: Defendant Abraham Carlisle took a commission from the King of Great Britain and granted passes in enemy-occupied territory. The indictment alleged multiple forms of treason but lacked specific details about an overt act beyond these allegations.
Disposition: Conviction upheld.

court: Philadelphia Court of Oyer and Terminer
summary:
Issue: Admissibility of out-of-court confessions as evidence in treason trials.
Holding: Out-of-court confessions are admissible to corroborate treason established by two witnesses but not sufficient alone for conviction.
Key Facts: Roberts was accused of enlisting and persuading others to enlist in the British army. His confession about communicating with an enemy supporter was admitted as corroboration, though it did not independently prove treason.
Disposition: Conviction upheld; motion for new trial denied.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: Whether the United States can be considered a body corporate for legal purposes and whether an indictment for forgery requires proof that someone was actually defrauded.
Holding: The court held that the United States became a body corporate upon its association and that it is sufficient in cases of fraud if the act has the potential to prejudice, without requiring actual harm to be proven.
Key Facts: Cornelius Sweers was convicted of forgery. The defense argued that the U.S. was not a legal entity at the time of the crime and that no actual fraud had occurred. The court disagreed on both points.
Disposition: Conviction upheld; Sweers sentenced to fines, imprisonment, and public humiliation.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: Whether a government official's fraudulent practice in selling bread at less than the marked weight constitutes an indictable offense against the public.
Holding: The court held that the defendant’s actions were injurious to the public and constituted an indictable offense, given the ease of perpetrating fraud due to customs allowing barrels of bread to be taken without reweighing.
Key Facts: Defendant was a government official who sold bread at less than the marked weight, taking advantage of the custom where barrels of bread were accepted based on marked weights without further verification.
Disposition: Conviction upheld; offense is indictable.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: Whether a remainder in fee is barred by the attainder of an ancestor who held an estate-tail under a will.
Holding: The court held that John Parrock took an estate-tail under the devise, and his land was forfeited upon attainder. Therefore, Abel James's claim to a vested remainder in fee was dismissed.
Key Facts: Abel James claimed a remainder in fee after John Parrock’s estate. The will gave property to John Parrock with the condition that it pass to issue if none reached age 21. John Parrock died without issue, and his land was forfeited due to attainder.
Disposition: Claim dismissed.

court: Pennsylvania High Court of Errors and Appeals
document_citation: Montgomery v. Henry et al. 1 Dall. 49, 47 (1780)
summary:
Issue: Admiralty jurisdiction and the right of ship owners to dismiss a captain without cause
Holding: The court affirmed that admiralty courts lack jurisdiction to hear cases involving actions taken within port jurisdictions; dismissed captains may be entitled to damages in common-law courts if dismissal was a wanton abuse.
Key Facts: Captain Montgomery sued his former employers for dismissing him while the ship lay idle due to weather delays. The owners claimed they had the right to dismiss the captain at will.
Disposition: Affirmed with costs

case_name: The Claim of Jacobs v. Adams, Executor
court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: The Claim of Jacobs v. Adams, Executor 1 Dall. 52, 50 (1781)
summary:
Issue: Whether interest should be allowed when a claimant paid purchase money to executors based on an agreement for the sale of land, but the testator's will appointing those executors was later set aside due to undue influence?
Holding: No interest ought to be allowed under these circumstances.
Key Facts: Flowers and Jacobs entered into a land sale agreement; Flowers died and Jacobs paid purchase money to his executors; the will appointing these executors was later invalidated for undue influence; Jacobs filed a claim to recover improperly paid money.
Disposition: Claim allowed for principal sum without interest.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: Whether Samuel Chapman could be considered a subject or inhabitant of Pennsylvania during a period when the government structure was in flux, affecting his status under treason laws.
Holding: The court held that despite the absence of a fully functioning government from December 1776 to March 1777, individuals were still bound by allegiance to the state once its sovereignty and governance were re-established.
Key Facts: Chapman withdrew from Pennsylvania in December 1776 when the state's government was not operating. The new constitution was formed on November 28, 1776, but the executive council did not meet until March 4, 1777. Chapman was charged with treason for aiding enemies of the state.
Disposition: Unknown

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: Whether an executive proclamation requiring a specific individual to surrender himself is sufficiently clear when it misidentifies the person's township.
Holding: The court held that the executive council must be bound by strict descriptions similar to those required in indictments. A misidentification of the accused’s township renders the proclamation unclear and ineffective.
Key Facts: Joshua Buffington was named in a government proclamation but with an incorrect township designation (East Bradford instead of West Bradford). The court found this discrepancy insufficient to establish identity.
Disposition: The defendant was acquitted due to lack of clear identification in the proclamation.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: Competency of witnesses when they have a potential interest or bias due to expected benefits from the outcome of the case.
Holding: A witness who acknowledges an expectation of personal benefit if one party wins is considered biased and thus incompetent to testify.
Key Facts: Scull, a creditor-like witness in McVeaugh v. Goods, admitted expecting some reward if the goods were condemned, leading the court to deem him an improper witness due to potential bias.
Disposition: The witness was declared incompetent.

case_name: Rapp v. Le Blanc et al.
court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: Rapp v. Le Blanc et al. 1 Dall. 63, 60 (1781)
summary:
Issue: The admissibility of an informer as a witness in cases involving seized goods and the legal requirements for certification by judges.
Holding: The court held that the informer cannot be a witness due to interest, even if they release their right to a portion of the seized goods. The judge must have authority under the act of assembly to certify; otherwise, the informer remains liable for costs. An informer cannot call a witness to disprove testimony contradicted by another witness.
Key Facts: Goods were seized as British imports in Pennsylvania. Legal proceedings involved an informer seeking recovery and claimants contesting the seizure. The act of assembly governed certification procedures and cost liabilities.
Disposition: Unknown

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: Validity and admissibility of a deed when executed with an ink seal and sworn to by one witness before a magistrate.
Holding: The court held that a deed under an ink seal and attested by one witness before a magistrate is valid, but this holding was later disapproved in subsequent cases.
Key Facts: A deed was signed with an ink seal and witnessed by one person before a magistrate. The validity of such a deed was challenged.
Disposition: The court initially accepted the deed as valid under the rule allowing proof by one witness, but this ruling was later overturned.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: Presumption regarding interlineations in deeds and their effect on legal documents
Holding: An interlineation made after the execution of a deed is presumed to be fraudulent unless proven otherwise; it will invalidate the deed even for immaterial alterations.
Key Facts: Morris's Lessee v. Vanderen addressed the validity of an altered deed where an interlineation was contested. Chief Justice McKean established a rule that post-execution interlineations are presumed to be fraudulent and invalid, unless proven otherwise by evidence such as similar ink and handwriting or conduct of parties.
Disposition: Verdict for the plaintiff on one-third of the lot in question; verdict for the defendant on the other two-thirds.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: Respublica v. Shryber et al. 1 Dall. 68, 66 (1782)
summary:
Issue: Unknown
Holding: Unknown
Key Facts: Unknown
Disposition: Unknown

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: Whether a deed must be recorded in the Rolls Office to be admissible as evidence under Pennsylvania law.
Holding: The court held that a sheriff's deed of sale, though not recorded in the Rolls Office as required by statute, could still be read in evidence if it was acknowledged and registered in the prothonotary’s office.
Key Facts: Sergeant and Lngersoll opposed reading a deed in evidence because it was not recorded according to the act of assembly. The court allowed the deed to be read despite this, citing its acknowledgment and registration in the prothonotary's office as sufficient recording under the law.
Disposition: The court overruled objections and allowed the deed to be admitted as evidence.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: Wilcox et al. v. Henry 1 Dall. 69, 68 (1782)
summary:
Issue: The legal status of debts and property rights between American citizens and British subjects post-Revolutionary War.
Holding: At the end of the war, all rights and credits that the subjects of either power had against the other are revived according to international law and usage.
Key Facts: During the evacuation of Philadelphia by the British army, there was a debate over whether property belonging to the king or his subjects should go to the United States or Pennsylvania. It was agreed that public property would belong to the U.S., while private property would remain with individuals in Pennsylvania. The case involved concerns about American citizens being deprived of debts due from abroad while still owing debts to British subjects.
Disposition: Verdict for the plaintiffs.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: Whether a cestui que trust can bring an ejectment action when the trustee refuses to act.
Holding: The court held that without an equitable remedy or the ability for the cestui que trust to sue in their own name, they would lack a legal remedy against an obstinate trustee.
Key Facts: Atlee J. ruled on the case; McKean C.J. was absent; no court of equity existed in Pennsylvania at the time.
Disposition: Unknown

court: Philadelphia Court of Oyer and Terminer
document_citation: Respublica v. Mesca et al. 1 Dall. 73, 72 (1783)
summary:
Issue: Whether British statutes extended to Pennsylvania by judicial usage or legislative intent should be applied in criminal cases.
Holding: The court held that the statute allowing a trial per medietatem linguae (trial by half of one language) was still applicable despite doubts about its original extension, due to precedent and legislative silence.
Key Facts: A motion for a trial per medietatem linguae was made; the court considered historical usage and legislative acts regarding British statute extensions in Pennsylvania.
Disposition: The court granted the request for a trial per medietatem linguae.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
document_citation: McCarty v. Nixon et al. 1 Dall. 77, 75 (1784)
summary:
Issue: When does an action become 'depending' for legal purposes?
Holding: An action becomes 'depending' when the original writ is purchased and issued out of the same court where it will be returned, not before.
Key Facts: Defendant not in court; motion denied due to timing issues with the action's dependency status.
Disposition: Motion denied.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Unknown
Holding: Unknown
Key Facts: Unknown
Disposition: Unknown

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
document_citation: Carlisle et ux. v. Cunningham 1 Dall. 81, 79 (1784)
summary:
Issue: Whether the court could compel a plaintiff who obtained possession of property through elegit to surrender it back to the defendant upon payment of principal, interest, and costs.
Holding: The court declined to rule on the motion in a summary manner due to its reluctance to expand jurisdiction beyond what was strictly necessary. The principal question remained undetermined.
Key Facts: Plaintiffs obtained possession of property through an elegit process; defendant sought return of the property upon payment of outstanding amounts; house-rent was rising rapidly, making the remainder of the term valuable to both parties.
Disposition: The rule was discharged, and the court left the principal question undetermined.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Whether to set aside verdicts of juries or reports of referees on frivolous grounds.
Holding: The court will not overturn verdicts or referee reports unless there is a clear lack of proper evidence before the jury or referee.
Key Facts: Unknown
Disposition: Rule discharged.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Whether a court may inquire beyond the written instrument when the defendant claims that the bond was delivered without consideration.
Holding: The court held that it cannot inquire beyond the written instrument and must recognize the bond as conclusive evidence of the contract, given the act of assembly allowing delay of execution upon tender of interest and costs.
Key Facts: Defendant argued that inquiry should be allowed to show the bond was delivered without consideration. Court rejected this, citing potential for infinite trouble and confusion if such inquiries were permitted.
Disposition: Judgment against defendant; motion denied.

case_name: Rodman et al., Executors, v. Hoops's Executor
court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: Rodman et al., Executors, v. Hoops's Executor 1 Dall. 85, 83 (1784)
summary:
Issue: Admissibility of ledger entries as evidence and distinction between original records and transcripts.
Holding: Ledger entries may be admitted if they appear to be original records made contemporaneously with the transactions; however, this ruling is case-specific and not precedent-setting. The jury must determine whether the entries are originals or transcripts.
Key Facts: A ledger was offered as evidence in a dispute over payment of an old note. The court allowed its submission but left it to the jury to decide if it was an original record or a transcript, emphasizing that only original records are admissible.
Disposition: The case was remanded to the jury for determination based on the ledger's nature as either an original document or a transcript.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: Proper procedure and legality of criminal proceedings leading to capital punishment under Pennsylvania law.
Holding: The Supreme Court of Pennsylvania held that the execution warrant could not be legally issued due to procedural defects in the outlawry process, which did not comply with statutory requirements.
Key Facts: Aaron Doan was convicted through an outlawry procedure. The sheriff's return lacked specific details required by law regarding proclamations and the timing of actions. Council determined that these omissions rendered the proceedings invalid.
Disposition: The court resolved that a warrant could not be legally issued for executing Aaron Doan due to procedural errors in his outlawry.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: Whether possession for over twenty years along with a deed is sufficient to validate the transfer of property without further proof of the deed's execution.
Holding: The court held that long-term possession (more than twenty years) under a deed can be sufficient evidence to validate the deed, thus dispensing with the need for additional proof of its due execution.
Key Facts: A dispute over property ownership where the claimant has possessed the land for more than two decades based on a deed. The opposing party challenged the validity of the deed without producing the underlying judgment and execution documents.
Disposition: Affirmed possession based on long-term use of the deed, indicating that further proof was unnecessary.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: Requirements for the validity of a will devising real estate in Pennsylvania
Holding: A will devising real estate does not need to be sealed, all subscribing witnesses do not have to prove execution, proof of the will does not have to be made by subscribing witnesses, and the will does not require subscriptions by the witnesses.
Key Facts: Unknown
Disposition: Affirmed the legal standards for will validation without specific factual details

case_name: Talbot, qui tam, &c., v. The Commanders and Owners of three Brigs
court: Pennsylvania High Court of Errors and Appeals
document_citation: Talbot, qui tam, &c., v. The Commanders and Owners of three Brigs 1 Dall. 95, 97 (1784)
summary:
Issue: Whether the suit was instituted in the prize court or the instance court of admiralty.
Holding: The court held that the suit was not properly instituted in the prize court but rather in the instance court of admiralty.
Key Facts: Lord Mansfield distinguished between the prize court and the instance court; the case involved a qui tam action against commanders and owners of three brigs; the court emphasized the distinct procedures of each court, with the prize court focusing on proceedings in rem (against the captured ship and goods).
Disposition: The court concluded that appeals from the prize court should go to Congress, while those from the instance court should go to the Pennsylvania High Court of Errors and Appeals.

court: Philadelphia Court of Oyer and Terminer
summary:
Issue: Competency of a witness who is the victim of the crime to testify in criminal proceedings.
Holding: The court held that a person defrauded or injured by the defendant's actions is a competent witness and may testify in criminal proceedings.
Key Facts: Chief Justice McKean cited previous cases (Respublica v. Ross, Rex v. Chapman) where victims were allowed to testify after legal arguments regarding their competency as witnesses.
Disposition: Unknown

court: Philadelphia Court of Oyer and Terminer
summary:
Issue: Whether threatening a foreign diplomat constitutes an offense under the law of nations and municipal law.
Holding: The defendant was found guilty of violating the law of nations by threatening a foreign diplomat, which is considered an insult to the sovereign and detrimental to international relations.
Key Facts: Defendant threatened bodily harm to a French consul in his residence; court viewed this as an offense against the law of nations and Pennsylvania's municipal law.
Disposition: Convicted; fined one hundred French crowns, imprisoned for two years, required to post bail and keep the peace for seven years.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Uncertainty in a referee's report regarding the amount due and its status at the time of reporting
Holding: The court set aside the report due to uncertainty about whether the sum mentioned was still owed at the time of the report.
Key Facts: Referees reported that £751 was due on March 3rd, several months before the meeting; this created ambiguity as to the amount's status at reporting time.
Disposition: Set aside the report

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
document_citation: Gerard v. Basse et al. 1 Dall. 119, 121 (1784)
summary:
Issue: Whether a warrant of attorney executed by one partner binds both partners when it is not under seal but relates to mercantile transactions.
Holding: The act of one partner in matters relating to joint trade should be deemed the act of both, although it be the signing bills of exchange or receipts. However, this rule does not apply to sealed instruments unless executed with the presence or authority of the other partner.
Key Facts: Gerard sought to set aside a judgment against Basse and Soyer based on a warrant of attorney executed by Soyer alone. The court ruled that while mercantile acts of one partner bind both, this does not extend to sealed instruments without explicit involvement of the other partner.
Disposition: Judgment set aside as to Basse; confirmed as to Soyer.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: How to compute interest when a partial payment is made before the due date of the principal.
Holding: The court held that a partial payment should first be applied to any accrued interest before being applied to the principal. This rule prevents overpayment of interest and ensures accurate calculation of remaining debt.
Key Facts: A bond existed for £100 with annual six percent interest. A payment of £50 was made after six months but before the due date of the principal. The court ruled that this payment should first cover accrued interest, then reduce the principal.
Disposition: The case established a rule that partial payments are to be applied first to accrued interest and then to the principal, overturning previous rulings.

case_name: Wharton et al. v. Morris et al.
court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: Wharton et al. v. Morris et al. 1 Dall. 125, 127 (1785)
summary:
Issue: Jurisdictional limitations on common-law courts to interpret equitable agreements and define 'current money' under Pennsylvania law.
Holding: The court lacks authority to determine the nature of 'current money' without legislative definition; thus, the jury must decide based on the evidence provided.
Key Facts: A bond payable in current money of Pennsylvania was at issue. The court noted historical attempts to establish a court of chancery and the lack of clear legal definitions for 'current money'.
Disposition: The jury found for the plaintiffs with damages and costs, as the court could not definitively interpret 'current money' without legislative guidance.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: Whether the defendant must confess lease, entry and ouster for the whole tenements laid in the declaration.
Holding: The defendant must confess lease, entry and ouster for the whole tenements as claimed by the plaintiff.
Key Facts: A legal action where the plaintiff (Wilson's Lessee) claims rights to certain tenements against the defendant (Campbell). The court ruled on this matter in Carlisle N.P. before Chief Justice McKean on May 16, 1785.
Disposition: Judgment for the plaintiff.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
document_citation: Scottin v. Stanley et al. 1 Dall. 129, 132 (1785)
summary:
Issue: Liability of ship owners for work performed after they became owners.
Holding: Owners who become such after work is performed are liable if the work was done on their credit and they avowedly acted as owners during the work period.
Key Facts: Ship repairs were completed after defendants became owners; plaintiffs proved that the repairs were conducted on defendants' credit and with their acknowledgment of ownership.
Disposition: Verdict for the plaintiff.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Unknown
Holding: The court inclined to agree with Lewis's position on the first point but did not render a formal judgment to allow the parties to resolve the matter through a new reference.
Key Facts: Parties sought resolution through legal proceedings; court favored Lewis’s argument initially but deferred final decision to enable settlement via renewed arbitration.
Disposition: The case was referred back to the parties for a new reference or arbitration, and no formal judgment was issued.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Whether the proceedings in a bail-bond suit should be stayed when the defendant pleads issueably in the original action and consents to the judgment on the bail-bond standing as security.
Holding: The court held that the rule for staying the bail-bond suit was made absolute, indicating that the proceedings would not be stayed under the proposed conditions.
Key Facts: Defendant filed an affidavit of just defense and sought a stay of the bail-bond suit in exchange for paying costs and consenting to the judgment on the bail-bond as security. After arguments and legal citations, the court ruled against staying the proceedings.
Disposition: The rule was made absolute, denying the request to stay the bail-bond suit.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Whether a recognizance is a lien on lands by common law or through statutory authority in Pennsylvania.
Holding: A recognizance does not create a lien on lands by common law; such liens exist only under specific statutory provisions or long-standing usage.
Key Facts: Chief Justice Shipper held that a recognizance is not a lien on the lands of special bail based on general understanding and practice. The court upheld this precedent, noting it would cause significant disruption to honest purchasers if overturned.
Disposition: The plaintiff's claim was limited to the date of judgment on the scire facias.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Whether an execution can issue based on a warrant of attorney that recites a bond before the payment period specified in the bond has expired.
Holding: The court held that the warrant of attorney is subservient to the bond and the execution cannot be issued until the time for payment given in the bond has passed.
Key Facts: A warrant of attorney referencing a bond was presented; the bond's payment period had not yet expired.
Disposition: The execution was set aside.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Whether a lower court can order an amendment to a verdict after an appeal has been filed.
Holding: The court below may order an amendment to the record or verdict while it remains with them, even after an error is brought.
Key Facts: Unknown
Disposition: Affirmed; the lower court's ability to amend was upheld.

case_name: Jackson v. Mason; Jackson v. Keely
court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: Jackson v. Mason; Jackson v. Keely 1 Dall. 135, 140 (1785)
summary:
Issue: Whether a defendant who is primarily liable can be separately tried from a defendant who is secondarily liable when they are parties to the same legal action.
Holding: The court held that both defendants should not be separately tried when they are involved in the same legal action, especially when one (the maker) is liable over to the other (the indorser).
Key Facts: Jackson brought actions on a note of hand where Mason was the maker and Keely the indorser. Both were considered interested parties in the same legal action.
Disposition: The trials were postponed due to the interdependence of the defendants' liabilities.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: Whether the Orphans' Court has discretion to appoint guardians for minors under fourteen years old or if such appointment must be made according to common law rules (guardian in socage).
Holding: The Orphans’ Court has legal discretion to assign guardianship of minors under fourteen years old, choosing persons of the same religious persuasion and good repute who are approved by the orphan.
Key Facts: Intestate left seven minor children; their mother remarried. The Orphans' Court appointed Enoch Edwards as guardian over objections that the legal guardian should be the next-of-kin or the mother herself under common law rules.
Disposition: The order of the Orphans’ Court confirming the appointment of guardians was upheld.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: Whether a deed conveying property 'fully, freely, absolutely and clearly' in consideration of natural affection passes a fee simple or an estate for life.
Holding: The deed is construed as a covenant to stand seised to uses, passing a fee simple interest.
Key Facts: Johannes Vandegrift conveyed land to his son Abraham 'in consideration of natural affection.' The deed lacked explicit words of inheritance but included phrases indicating absolute and clear grant. Legal arguments centered on whether the deed should be treated as a common-law conveyance or a covenant to stand seised to uses.
Disposition: Judgment for the plaintiff; the deed conveyed a fee simple interest.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: Whether an assignee's interest in a chose in action is protected by law and whether the original plaintiff remains liable for costs when the suit is brought for the benefit of the assignee.
Holding: The court recognized that the beneficial interest vested in the assignee of a chose in action is fully protected, and the assignee is made liable to set-off and costs as if the action were instituted in their name. However, the original plaintiff remains not liable for costs when they take no part in the suit and the assignment occurred before the lawsuit was filed.
Key Facts: General Forman’s bond fide assignment; court's refusal to allow collusive settlements between original parties; recognition of assignee’s beneficial interest; liability of nominal plaintiff for costs.
Disposition: The court ordered the jury to be sworn and recognized the assignee’s rights, while noting that the original plaintiff is not liable for costs under certain conditions.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: Whether a superior military officer is liable for the debts incurred by an inferior officer when the funds were used for army purposes.
Holding: The court inclined to think that the superior officer would be liable if the inferior officer had authority to contract and applied the money for army use, provided the superior had sufficient public funds to discharge it.
Key Facts: Unknown
Disposition: Inclined towards liability under specific conditions

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
document_citation: Woods v. Courter et al. 1 Dall. 141, 146 (1785)
summary:
Issue: Admissibility of an affidavit regarding ship ownership and its use in evidence against defendants.
Holding: The court allowed the affidavit to be read as evidence against the defendants after argument.
Key Facts: Affidavit from naval officer's records stating joint ownership of a ship; defendant not present (nonest inventus); plaintiff eventually suffered nonsuit.
Disposition: Unknown

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Whether a debtor can petition for discharge under insolvent laws after the court has adjourned its session for hearing insolvent debtors.
Holding: The court held that the debtor's application was too late because it was made after the court had adjourned its session for hearing insolvent debtors.
Key Facts: Debtor in confinement under a magistrates execution petitioned for discharge under insolvent laws post-adjournment. Court remanded due to timing of the petition.
Disposition: Remand

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Whether a mortgagee can tack subsequent debts to a mortgage debt when seeking recovery through legal proceedings.
Holding: The court held that under Pennsylvania law, a mortgagee cannot tack subsequent debts to a mortgage debt; the remedy is confined to recovering the principal and interest due on the mortgage.
Key Facts: A rule was sought to show cause why land should not be sold for payment of the principal and interest due on a mortgage. The court emphasized that Pennsylvania law differs from English law, limiting recovery to the principal and interest without allowing tacking of subsequent debts.
Disposition: Rule made absolute; the mortgagee could only recover the principal and interest, with no allowance for subsequent debts.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
document_citation: Brown v. Scott et al. 1 Dall. 145, 150 (1785)
summary:
Issue: Whether parties can consolidate separate legal actions through agreement and whether arbitrators have authority to consolidate claims without defendant's consent.
Holding: The court confirmed the report but noted doubts about the arbitrators' authority to consolidate claims. The decision implies that consolidation by agreement alone may not be sufficient, and arbitrators lack authority to consolidate without explicit consent.
Key Facts: Five separate actions with five rules of reference; referees attempted to consolidate despite potential legal limitations; court confirmed report but acknowledged internal disagreement on arbitrator authority.
Disposition: Confirmed the report as presented by the plaintiff.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Whether a protest for non-acceptance alone is sufficient to recover money from the drawer of a bill of exchange when the holder voluntarily pays the amount without seeking legal recourse.
Holding: The court ruled that since the plaintiff voluntarily paid the defendant both principal and damages, the plaintiff cannot recover the money back regardless of whether the protest for non-acceptance was legally sufficient.
Key Facts: A bill of exchange drawn by Benjamin Harrison & Company on a house in France was refused acceptance. A protest for non-acceptance was made but not acted upon; instead, the plaintiff voluntarily paid the defendant both principal and damages.
Disposition: Judgment for the defendant.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Whether a debtor can be discharged under an insolvent debtor relief act if they apply after the first three days of the term.
Holding: No, the court will not discharge debtors who apply after the initial three-day window to avoid continuous engagement with such cases and potential delays for other legal matters.
Key Facts: The defendant applied for discharge outside the first three days of the term; the Prothonotary noted that the writ of execution must be returnable at the term when the debtor applies.
Disposition: Dismissal of the petition.

court: Philadelphia Court of Oyer and Terminer
summary:
Issue: Whether evidence allowing for speculative justifications beyond clear legal boundaries is admissible in court.
Holding: The court held that such evidence was inadmissible as it amounted to mere opinion and would allow justification for actions beyond legal limits, undermining public property rights.
Key Facts: Chief Justice McKean ruled on the admissibility of evidence in a case involving a wharf's encroachment on public space. The defense sought to introduce evidence suggesting partial justifications for the encroachment, which the court rejected.
Disposition: The evidence was deemed inadmissible.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Unknown
Holding: The Commonwealth was not entitled to a preference based on the given facts.
Key Facts: Unknown
Disposition: Judgment for the defendant (Hamelin)

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Whether Lazarus Barnet was subject to foreign attachment or domestic attachment based on his residency status at the time of granting the writ.
Holding: The court held that Lazarus Barnet was not subject to foreign attachments because he was a resident within the state, despite arguments suggesting otherwise. The decision is grounded in the legislative history and uniform construction of Pennsylvania’s attachment laws from 1724 to 1774.
Key Facts: Lazarus Barnet's residency status at the time of granting the domestic attachment was critical; the court relied on historical acts (4 Ann., c. 28, 9 Geo. I, c. 3, and 14 Geo. III, c. 5) to determine that he fell under domestic attachment laws.
Disposition: Foreign attachments against Lazarus Barnet were dissolved.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Requirements for service of a summons and filing of a declaration in order to obtain a default judgment.
Holding: To entitle a plaintiff to a default judgment by service of a summons on the defendant, it must be served ten days before the return date. If the record does not specify this timeframe or is unclear, the court will reverse the judgment unless the declaration was filed before judgment and the service was generally noted as completed.
Key Facts: Service requirements; timing of summons service; filing of declaration.
Disposition: Reversed if service timing is insufficiently documented; presumption in favor of proper service if record does not specify timeframe.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
document_citation: Vienne v. McCarty, surviving Partner 1 Dall. 154, 159 (1785)
summary:
Issue: Whether a creditor can sue a surviving partner for debts incurred by the deceased partner before the partnership formation.
Holding: No, because the debt was the separate obligation of the deceased partner and no lien or privity exists between the creditor and the surviving partner.
Key Facts: James Cummins drew a bill of exchange; McCarty is the surviving partner. The action is based on money recovered by McCarty as surviving partner from other parties, not directly from the debtor's estate.
Disposition: Judgment for defendant.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Proper procedure and liability in replevin actions
Holding: The sheriff must allow reasonable time for the defendant to find security; bail are liable up to the bond penalty; a clause providing for return of goods if adjudged by law is illegal, with judgment only for damages and costs.
Key Facts: Sheriff's duty to provide time for securing property; liability of bail; illegality of clauses returning goods based on legal determination; judgment limited to damages and costs.
Disposition: Rule discharged.

case_name: Taylor v. Knox et al.; Finlayson v. Knox et al.
court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
document_citation: Taylor v. Knox et al.; Finlayson v. Knox et al. 1 Dall. 158, 163 (1785)
summary:
Issue: Whether a defendant's residence is sufficient under Pennsylvania law to allow domestic attachment when it is less than twelve months and whether foreign creditors can attach assets.
Holding: The court held that a shorter residency period does not qualify for domestic attachment if the circumstances do not indicate an unequivocal intention to continue residence. The court also ruled that affidavits from abroad should be sufficient proof in cases of attachments, similar to English practice before the revolution.
Key Facts: Knox resided in Pennsylvania for eight or nine months without establishing a clear intent to remain permanently; his family situation did not indicate long-term residency.
Disposition: Motion to dissolve the attachments discharged.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Whether the insured complied with a warranty in an insurance policy.
Holding: The court held that the insured did not comply with the warranty条款，但为了保持回答的简洁和符合要求，我将省略对“holding”部分的具体法律解释。根据摘要文本，“holding”可以总结为：被告（保险人）胜诉。
Key Facts: Underwriters required a warranty in the insurance contract; the owners did not comply with this warranty.
Disposition: Judgment for the defendant.

case_name: Marriot et ux v. Davey et al., Executors
court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
document_citation: Marriot et ux v. Davey et al., Executors 1 Dall. 164, 169 (1786)
summary:
Issue: Whether a settlement of an administration account is conclusive in subsequent legal actions.
Holding: The court determined that the settlement was not conclusive and new auditors should be appointed upon plea of want of assets.
Key Facts: Unknown
Disposition: Judgment for defendants, as the settlement was not deemed conclusive.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Authority of the court to grant a rule to take depositions before the return of the writ when the defendant is in confinement and material witnesses are about to leave the state.
Holding: The court has authority to grant such a rule, despite some doubt expressed in other cases. The practice in Philadelphia courts uniformly supports this authority.
Key Facts: Defendant was in confinement; material witnesses were about to leave the state; writ not returnable until next term.
Disposition: Rule granted.

case_name: Sliver, Plaintiff in error, v. Shelback
court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: Sliver, Plaintiff in error, v. Shelback 1 Dall. 165, 171 (1786)
summary:
Issue: Whether an infant's appearance in court without a guardian constitutes a valid legal act.
Holding: The court held that an infant’s appearance in court without a guardian does not constitute a valid legal act; such appearances are erroneous and invalid under the common law.
Key Facts: An infant appeared in court to defend a suit without being represented by a guardian or next friend, as required by statute.
Disposition: The court ruled that the appearance was erroneous.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: Pirate, alias Belt, v. Dalby 1 Dall. 167, 172 (1786)
summary:
Issue: Whether a person born in Virginia to enslaved parents is free or remains enslaved upon entering Pennsylvania.
Holding: The court held that the plaintiff, born into slavery in Virginia, remained enslaved under Pennsylvania law due to the legal principle that status follows condition of the father.
Key Facts: Plaintiff was born in Virginia to enslaved parents; entered Pennsylvania; no mention of voluntary servitude or contract.
Disposition: Verdict for the defendant.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: Kunckel et al. v. Baker 1 Dall. 169, 175 (1786)
summary:
Issue: Whether filing a declaration constitutes a waiver of the right to special bail.
Holding: Filing a declaration does not waive the right to special bail.
Key Facts: Plaintiff's counsel argued that defendant should not lose the right to special bail by filing a declaration, which was seen as accepting common appearance. Court agreed with this motion.
Disposition: Court ruled in favor of plaintiff’s counsel, directing defendant to give special bail within two months or face procedendo.

case_name: Moore's Lessee v. Few et al.
court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: Moore's Lessee v. Few et al. 1 Dall. 170, 175 (1786)
summary:
Issue: Whether a non-juror could legally devise land and tenements under Pennsylvania law in effect as of June 13, 1777.
Holding: The court held that a non-juror was incapable of devising lands and tenements due to legal disabilities imposed by the act of assembly passed on June 13, 1777.
Key Facts: Testator John Hunt died as a non-juror; plaintiff's title derived from a devise by John Hunt; defendants claimed under the heir-at-law.
Disposition: Judgment for the defendants.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Whether property in goods for which replevin issued was vested in the plaintiff.
Holding: The court held that the property in the goods was not vested in the plaintiff.
Key Facts: Unknown
Disposition: Verdict for the defendant.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: Grier et al. v. Grier 1 Dall. 173, 178 (1786)
summary:
Issue: Validity of an arbitration award where payment is directed to parties who are strangers (executors) under the condition that they can be identified.
Holding: The court held that the award was valid because it could be construed with sufficient certainty and finality, despite the payment being directed to executors who were not direct parties. The arbitrators' intention was clear from the context of the dispute over loan-office certificates.
Key Facts: Arbitration involved a dispute about exchanging loan-office certificates; award ordered defendant to pay £1751 to John Grier's executors for loss sustained by exchange; executors were not named but could be identified easily; two essentials in awards are certainty and finality.
Disposition: Affirmed the validity of the arbitration award.

case_name: Kerlin's Lessee v. Bull et al.
court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: Kerlin's Lessee v. Bull et al. 1 Dall. 175, 180 (1786)
summary:
Issue: Whether a devise to a child who dies before reaching a specified age is a lapsed devise or vests immediately upon the death of the testator, affecting distribution among surviving siblings.
Holding: The court held that the estate vested immediately in the devisee upon the death of the testator and then descended equally among the surviving brother and sisters when the devisee died intestate before reaching the specified age. The court also ruled that the act of assembly did not apply to this case as it pertained only to lands coming by descent, not by will.
Key Facts: Testator devised land to his son John until a certain age; John died under age and intestate while his mother survived; surviving siblings contested distribution.
Disposition: Judgment for the defendants.

court: Pennsylvania High Court of Errors and Appeals
document_citation: Purviance et al. v. Angus 1 Dall. 180, 185 (1786)
summary:
Issue: Whether Captain Angus can be held liable for taking the brig Betsey as a prize without probable cause, when acting in conjunction with other captains on a joint cruise.
Holding: Captain Angus is not liable unless there was gross negligence or intentional misconduct. His actions were warranted by maritime law governing joint cruises and he reasonably relied on the judgment of his fellow captains.
Key Facts: Angus was ordered to join two other vessels on a cruise; he took the brig Betsey as prize based on information from his colleagues; no evidence of gross negligence or intentional misconduct against him.
Disposition: Affirmed

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Whether it is proper for referees to conduct separate examinations of a witness produced by one party without allowing the opposing party an opportunity for cross-examination.
Holding: It is improper for referees to separately examine a witness produced by one party when the evidence relates to general points; the adverse parties should have the chance to cross-examine the witness.
Key Facts: Referees conducted separate examinations of a witness without allowing cross-examination by the opposing party. The court found this procedure improper and set aside the report.
Disposition: The report was set aside.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Whether a judgment can relate back to the first day of a term and thereby defeat a domestic attachment.
Holding: The court held that a subsequent judgment cannot relate back to the first day of a term to avoid the lien of an existing domestic attachment.
Key Facts: Legal actions under acts of 1723 and 1752; later act of 1807 altered provisions regarding trustees' vested estate rights post-attachment issuance; case involved arguments by Lewis and Ingersoll.
Disposition: The court did not render a formal judgment but clearly expressed its opinion on the issue.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Whether a report by referees should be invalidated due to alleged impropriety in their conduct during the reference process.
Holding: The court held that minor irregularities or perceived improprieties do not necessarily invalidate a referee's report, provided there is no clear evidence of partiality or corruption.
Key Facts: Referees made an unscheduled proposal to one party (Innes) outside the formal setting. The defendant alleged this as misbehavior warranting invalidation of the referees' report. Referees explained the incident, and the court found it did not rise to a level requiring intervention.
Disposition: Judgment entered for the plaintiff.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
document_citation: James et al. v. Allen 1 Dall. 188, 198 (1786)
summary:
Issue: Whether a discharge under another state’s insolvent law entitles the party to be liberated from imprisonment for debt in Pennsylvania.
Holding: A discharge under another state's general insolvent law does not entitle the party to a discharge from imprisonment in Pennsylvania, even if the debt was contracted in the state where the discharge occurred.
Key Facts: James et al. v. Allen involved a defendant discharged by an insolvent act of New Jersey who was imprisoned for debt in Pennsylvania. The court distinguished between laws with extra-territorial effect and those local to the issuing state, emphasizing that the New Jersey law lacked explicit territorial limitations.
Disposition: The court ruled against the application of comity principles that would extend reciprocal discharge benefits across states due to frequent inconveniences and uncertainties in practice.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: Whether an action can be brought by the payee of a bill of exchange against the drawer when the bill has been specially endorsed to another party for collection.
Holding: The court held that possession of the bill and protest, along with the endorsement on them, is sufficient evidence to allow the original payee to bring an action against the drawer without needing proof of a subsequent indorsee having received payment.
Key Facts: Plaintiff remitted collection of a protested English bill to correspondents in England with an endorsement 'Pay to the order of Clifford & Tyset.' Defendant moved for nonsuit, arguing that the action must be brought by the indorsees. The court allowed the plaintiff's suit.
Disposition: The court sustained the action and denied the defendant’s motion for a nonsuit.

case_name: Gerard v. La Coste et al.
court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
document_citation: Gerard v. La Coste et al. 1 Dall. 194, 206 (1787)
summary:
Issue: Whether a draft payable to a specific individual without additional terms (such as 'or order') is negotiable and can be assigned.
Holding: The court held that a bill or note must explicitly state its negotiability in its original form for it to be assignable. Absent such language, the instrument cannot be transferred to another party who may sue on it.
Key Facts: Plaintiff Gerard sought payment from defendants La Coste et al., claiming an assignment of a draft originally payable only to Bass & Soyer without additional terms allowing negotiation or assignment.
Disposition: Judgment for the defendant.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Unknown
Holding: The court granted leave to amend the pleading based on the premise that there was something to amend.
Key Facts: Unknown
Disposition: Granted leave to amend

case_name: Phile, qui tam, v. The Ship Anna
court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
document_citation: Phile, qui tam, v. The Ship Anna 1 Dall. 197, 209 (1787)
summary:
Issue: Whether a ship can be forfeited for the unlawful acts of its crew in unloading goods without proper customs entry.
Holding: Yes, under maritime law and Pennsylvania statutes, a vessel may be subject to forfeiture when its crew engages in illegal activities such as unlading goods without legal clearance.
Key Facts: Forty-two hampers of porter were unloaded from the ship Anna without prior entry at the collector’s office. The evidence showed that more goods were unloaded than officially entered, leading to a clear case of customs violation.
Disposition: Verdict in favor of the informants; the ship was condemned.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Whether assumpsit lies on a contract under seal modified by parol to the extent of abandoning the original contract.
Holding: Assumpsit lies upon a contract under seal if it has been so modified by parol as to amount to an abandonment of the original contract, but unless expressly abandoned or significantly altered, covenant is the only remedy for breach.
Key Facts: Unknown
Disposition: The court held that the law supports the defendant's position and ordered a new trial or nonsuit at the plaintiff’s election.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
summary:
Issue: Whether a covenant to repair binds an assignee and whether acts of God or enemy actions are within the scope of such a covenant.
Holding: The court held that the covenant to repair runs with the land and binds the assignee. The specific defense based on acts of God or enemy actions was not addressed in the provided excerpt, but the general holding supports the binding nature of the covenant against ordinary risks.
Key Facts: Pollard sued Shaffer as an assignee for breach of a lease covenant to keep premises in repair. Shaffer argued that such acts were beyond the covenant's scope due to their unforeseeable nature.
Disposition: Affirmed; no writ of error pursued.

court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
document_citation: Musgrove, qui tam, v. Gibbs 1 Dall. 216, 228 (1787)
summary:
Issue: Whether accepting new notes as payment for old debts with excessive interest constitutes usury.
Holding: The court ruled that the acceptance of new notes with compounded interest does not necessarily constitute usury if it is a fair purchase and not an attempt to evade legal restrictions on interest rates.
Key Facts: Plaintiff claimed defendant engaged in usurious practices by accepting new notes with excessive interest. Court left determination to jury whether defendant loaned money or purchased the note at a discount, considering risk of forgery.
Disposition: Unknown

case_name: Doane's Administrators v. Penhallow et al.
court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
document_citation: Doane's Administrators v. Penhallow et al. 1 Dall. 218, 230 (1787)
summary:
Issue: Whether a common law court has jurisdiction to determine disputes arising from the consequences of a vessel being taken as prize by a privateer.
Holding: The court lacks authority to adjudicate matters that are exclusively within admiralty jurisdiction, including questions arising from the consequences of a vessel’s capture as prize. The case involves the execution of a sentence issued by a maritime court and requires determination of whether the vessel was legally taken as prize, which is beyond the common law court's purview.
Key Facts: The action concerns the recovery of property seized as prize by a privateer; the original taking and subsequent condemnation in New Hampshire’s maritime court are central. The case hinges on the validity of sentences issued by different courts regarding the vessel’s status as prize or not.
Disposition: Judgment for defendants; attachments dissolved.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Whether a defendant who received money on behalf of another is liable for its return when tendered in depreciated currency.
Holding: The court held that the defendant was liable only for the amount actually received and could raise equitable defenses. The plaintiff waived tort claims and special damages, seeking only the money received by the defendant.
Key Facts: Defendant Hugg, as sheriff of Gloucester county, NJ, lent plaintiff Eastwick's funds to a neighbor with bond and mortgage in plaintiff’s name. Defendant collected interest payments but when principal was tendered in continental bills (depreciated currency), he referred mortgagor to the plaintiff. Court required proof that defendant acted as an agent before allowing him to testify under act of assembly.
Disposition: The court ordered a new trial due to legal clarity on liability limits and ruled against admitting certain testimony, leading to a verdict adjusting for currency depreciation.

court: Philadelphia County Court of Common Pleas
summary:
Issue: Whether a sheriff must allow a reasonable time for the defendant in replevin to find security before removing goods.
Holding: Yes, the sheriff must allow a reasonable time for the defendant to find security. If this time is unreasonably refused, the sheriff cannot justify removal of goods under the writ of replevin.
